yt_title: The Critical Middle: Scaling Project-Based Career Learning
speakers: Annalisa Stonner (Blue Springs School District); Courtney Reilly (Skillsline); Kate Parsons (CAPS Network); Renee Ilhardt (International Baccalaureate Organization); Corey Mohn (CAPS Network)
schedule_title: The Critical Middle: Scaling Project-Based Career Learning
schedule_description: Middle school is often overlooked as a critical developmental stage. Students are forming identities and interests, yet too often disengage academically just when curiosity should be taking flight. Britebound's research showcases this disconnect: 87% of middle schoolers say they want to connect their interests to potential careers and 70% of young people express that they would have been more engaged in school if learning had been connected to career interests.This session will discuss new...

yt_title: Does AI Expand Human Intelligence or Atrophy It？
speakers: James Donovan (OpenAI); Nataliya Kosmyna (MIT Media Lab); Alex Grodd (The Disagreement)
schedule_title: Does AI Expand Human Intelligence or Atrophy It?
schedule_description: AI systems are advancing faster than the humans expected to govern, use, and adapt alongside them. As models increasingly shape how people write, reason, learn, and decide, the central question is no longer technical. It is human: what kind of intelligence are we building, and what happens to our own in the process?Some see AI as a powerful cognitive amplifier, capable of expanding human potential when designed and used with intention. Others warn that offloading thinking too early or too...

yt_title: Skills, Degrees, or Both？ Making Sense of Signals
speakers: Donnell Butler (Prelude); Erica Bouris (Jobs for the Future); James Cryan (Willow Education); Jeff Bulanda (Jobs for the Future); Sheila Saremzadeh (BASTA); Judy Goldstein (Britebound); Julie Kreiss (Britebound)
schedule_title: Skills, Degrees, or Both? Making Sense of the Signals We Send Young People
schedule_description: Young people are navigating mixed and often conflicting messages about whether skills, credentials, or degrees matter most. Britebound's recent research captures this tension: parents' college pathway preference dropped from 74% to 58% since 2019, yet they're less supportive of nondegree credentials than no formal education at all. Young people, who cite parents as their biggest influencer, are caught in the middle. This session creates space for an honest, values-driven conversation about wh...

schedule_title: Are We in an AI Bubble?
schedule_description: Is AI the next great infrastructure cycle or the next great bubble?Enterprise demand is accelerating, hyperscalers are committing unprecedented capital to chips and data centers, and private markets are assigning historic valuations to AI startups. yt_title: From Big Tech to EdTech: Harnessing Human Intelligence in the AI Age
speakers: Philip Moyer (McGraw Hill); Rajen Sheth (Kyron Learning); Adam Freed (Bevel Inc)
schedule_title: From Big Tech to EdTech: Harnessing Human Intelligence in the AI Age
tags: AI/ML; Building and Investing in EdTech; Emerging Technologies; Higher Education
schedule_description: Philip Moyer, CEO of McGraw Hill, and Rajen Sheth, CEO of Kyron Learning, first worked together at Google. Today they lead companies approaching AI in education from very different starting points—one a global education solutions provider, the other an AI-native platform built for instruction and learning outcomes. Now collaborating on a new solution launching in 2026, they'll discuss what happens when large-scale learning content meets AI-powered instruction, how lessons from applying AI in...

yt_title: Agile or Fragile…Are Universities Stuck in the Year 2000？
speakers: Ebony Thomas (Year Up United | Grads of Life); John Katzman (Noodle Partners, PBC.); Kathleen deLaski (Education Design Lab); Mark Milliron (National University); Diego Arambula (Carnegie Foundation for the Advancement of Teaching)
schedule_title: Agile or Fragile…Are Universities Stuck in the Year 2000?
schedule_description: American universities were founded with a moral and civic purpose: to expand human potential and enable better lives. For generations, that purpose guided institutions as they prepared students not only for work, but for meaningful participation in society. Today, as the needs of learners and the economy evolve, that mission is being reexamined.At the center of this conversation is an important question: If higher education exists to improve lives, how well is it doing so today—and how might ...

yt_title: Durable Skills + AI Fluency: Preparing Youth for the Future of Work
speakers: Alex Kotran (The AI Education Project (aiEDU)); Isabelle Hau (Stanford Accelerator for Learning); John Branam (Bottom Line); Komal Dadlani (Lab4U); Zarek Drozda (Data Science 4 Everyone); Lisa Dughi (NAF)
schedule_title: Durable Skills + AI Fluency: Preparing Youth for the Future of Work
schedule_description: As artificial intelligence transforms the world of work, young people face an unprecedented challenge — and opportunity. The rise of automation and AI has made durable skills — like communication, creativity, problem-solving, adaptability, and ethical reasoning — even more essential. In fact, durable skills account for 70% of the most requested skills among employers. Technology will never replace human strengths, making it our responsibility to prepare young people to apply a human-centered...

yt_title: Is AI Amplifying Cognition or Dismantling It？
speakers: Benjamin Riley (Cognitive Resonance); Dan Meyer (Amplify); Joseph Davis (Ready For Mathematics and KAITLab); Joseph Wise (Distinctive Schools); Diane Tavenner (Futre.me)
schedule_title: Is AI Amplifying Cognition or Dismantling It?
schedule_description: AI is often framed as a productivity tool, but its most consequential role in education may be far more ambitious: the potential to serve as a true equalizer for students who have historically been least served by traditional systems. Used well, AI can expand access to high-quality support, personalize learning at scale, and help close opportunity gaps. Used poorly, it risks becoming another layer of convenience for adults while reinforcing the very inequities education seeks to solve.This...

yt_title: Beyond the Pilot: AI’s Real Impact on Learning Systems
Living & Learning With Aliens: The Complex Psychology of AI Anthropomorphism
speakers: Ann Wang (University of California, Oma Play); Matthew Biel (Georgetown University Thrive Center for Children, Families and Communities); Rachel Koblic (AI and Education Design Lab); Tanya Gamby (Southern New Hampshire University); Paul LeBlanc (Harvard University Graduate School of Education)
schedule_title: Living and Learning With Aliens: The Complex Psychological Terrain of AI Anthropomorphism
track: The Prime Time Stage powered by WGU
schedule_description: We live in a time when a large number of people report regular interactions with AI companions, turning to them for emotional and psychological support and even falling in love with them. This panel of experts explores what can be harmful, what can be healthy, and what new questions confront us as we learn to live with an alien intelligence that not only feels human, but preferably so for many people.

yt_title: Funding the Future of Student Support: Special Needs & Mental Health
speakers: John Rogers (TPG's Rise Fund); Kate Eberle Walker (Presence)
schedule_title: Funding the Future of Student Support: Special Needs & Mental Health
track: The Prime Time Stage powered by WGU
tags: Equality + Access; K-12; Policy; Wellness/Mental Health
schedule_description: Student needs are rising across K–12 and higher education—especially in special education and mental health—while budgets remain constrained, creating a widening gap between needs and available resources. At the same time, schools are navigating a broader conversation about the role of technology in the classroom, raising important questions about how to ensure students who rely on specialized services continue to benefit from innovations that expand access and support high-quality care. In...

yt_title: Non-Degree Paths: Hype or Hope？
speakers: Clayton Lord (SHRM Foundation); Erica Bouris (Jobs for the Future); Janelle Duray (Jobs for America's Graduates); Ebony Thomas (Year Up United | Grads of Life)
schedule_title: Non-Degree Paths: Hype or Hope?
schedule_description: As education and career decisions become more complex and costly, many young people are questioning whether a traditional degree is the right path. Yet, many lack the information and confidence to choose another way. Nearly half of young adults do not pursue a four-year degree, and many who do enroll never complete, often resulting in debt and missed opportunity.This panel highlights real-world proof points that short-term credentials (STCs) and nondegree pathways (NDPs) lead to meaningful,...
